cards.title: English has silent letters
cards.description:
Some English words have letters that are not pronounced, like:
knee
hour
knife

Silent letters are tricky.

cards.title: "e" is the most used letter
cards.description:
The letter E is the most commonly used letter in English words. You can find it in words like:
see
people

cards.title: English words from many languages
cards.description: English has borrowed words from many languages like French, Latin, Korean, and Japanese. That's why some English words can sound and look very different.

cards.title: One letter can have many sounds
cards.description:
English letters can sound different depending on the word.

Example:
a in "name"
a in "car"

cards.title: English is spoken worldwide
cards.description: English is used by millions of people worldwide for communication, school, travel, and entertainment. That's why it is one of the most important international languages today.

cards.title: Zero Was Not Always Used
cards.description: The number zero was not part of many ancient number systems. Today, it is one of the most important numbers in mathematics and technology.

cards.title: English Uses Special Number Words
cards.description: Some English numbers have unique names that do not follow regular patterns, such as eleven and twelve. This is why they must be memorized.

cards.title: Forty Has No "U"
cards.description: Many learners mistakenly write fourty, but the correct spelling is forty. It is one of the most common spelling mistakes in English.

cards.title: Numbers Are Everywhere
cards.description: English numbers are used in daily life for telling time, reading prices, sharing phone numbers, and talking about age.

cards.title: One Hundred Is a Milestone Number
cards.description: The number 100 is often used to represent a complete set, a perfect score, or an important achievement in many countries.

cards.title: English Uses Ordinal Numbers for Dates
cards.description: When reading dates, English uses ordinal numbers such as first, second, and third, instead of regular numbers.

cards.title: Not All Ordinal Numbers End the Same Way
cards.description: Most ordinal numbers end in -th, but some are unique, such as first, second, and third.

cards.title: Month Names Always Start with a Capital Letter
cards.description: In English, month names like January, April, and December always begin with a capital letter.

cards.title: Dates Can Be Written Differently
cards.description: Some countries write dates as Month-Day-Year, while others use Day-Month-Year. The pronunciation usually stays the same.

cards.title: Dates Are Used Every Day
cards.description: People use dates for birthdays, holidays, appointments, school schedules, and many other activities.

cards.title: Days Are Always Capitalized
cards.description: In English, the names of days always begin with a capital letter, such as Monday, Tuesday, and Sunday.

cards.title: Sunday Was Named After the Sun
cards.description: The word Sunday comes from the Sun, while Monday comes from the Moon.

cards.title: There Are 168 Hours in a Week
cards.description: A week has 7 days, and each day has 24 hours, making a total of 168 hours.

cards.title: Weekend Traditions Differ Around the World
cards.description: In many countries, the weekend is Saturday and Sunday, but some countries have different weekend schedules.

cards.title: Days Help Us Organize Time
cards.description: People use days of the week to plan school, work, meetings, holidays, and daily activities.

cards.title: July and August Are Named After Roman Leaders
cards.description: July was named after Julius Caesar, and August was named after Augustus Caesar.

cards.title: February Is the Shortest Month
cards.description: February usually has 28 days, but it has 29 days during a leap year.

cards.title: Month Names Always Start with a Capital Letter
cards.description: In English, month names such as January, May, and December always begin with a capital letter.

cards.title: There Are Four Seasons in a Year
cards.description: Many countries divide the year into four seasons: Spring, Summer, Autumn (Fall), and Winter.

cards.title: Some Holidays Are Named After Months
cards.description: For example, Halloween is celebrated in October, while Christmas is celebrated in December.
